title: Why the End of the Financial Year Is the Beginning of Your Capital Plan
excerpt: Most Australian SMEs treat June 30 as a compliance deadline. The sharper question is what your completed accounts tell you about funding the twelve months ahead.
content:
Most Australian business owners treat June 30 as a compliance milestone. The accounts need to be finalised, the returns need to be lodged, and the bookkeeper and accountant need the data they require to close out the year. That process is essential and it is running in offices across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria right now.

What tends not to happen alongside it is the capital conversation. The same data going to the accountant also contains the clearest picture you will have all year of how your business actually funds itself, where the gaps are, and what you need in place before the pressures of a new trading year arrive. That picture fades quickly once July's numbers start accumulating on top of it.

The businesses that start FY27 in a stronger financial position than they finished FY26 rarely got there through a single large decision made in January. They read their completed accounts in late June or early July, asked the right questions, and put the right structures in place before they needed them.

What a Completed Year of Accounts Actually Shows

Monthly management reports give you the direction of travel. The completed annual accounts give you something different: the actual pattern of how your business converts revenue into cash, how much working capital the growth consumed, and what the debt and facility position looks like after twelve months of real activity rather than projection.

Three numbers are worth isolating from the final accounts before the new year begins. The first is cash generated from operations, not profit but the actual cash the business produced from its trading activity across the year. The second is the change in net working capital: how much of that cash did the year's activity consume? If revenue grew but working capital grew faster, the gap had to come from somewhere. The third is peak borrowing versus year-end borrowing. What was the most the business drew on its facilities during the year, and how does that compare to the June 30 position? The difference tells you how much buffer your facilities actually provided and whether they were sized for the year you ran.

Together, these three numbers tell you whether your current funding structure still fits your business. The accounts also surface something that monthly reporting rarely makes visible: the distance between reported profit and actual cash. A business that posted solid profit across twelve months but finished June with a tight cash position is telling you something about its working capital cycle or the reinvestment demands of its growth. Understanding that gap is the starting point for any capital conversation worth having.

The Three Capital Questions Worth Answering This Week

The capital planning questions worth asking in late June are simpler than most business owners expect. The complexity is not in identifying the right questions. It is in making time to answer them before the new year starts and the questions get buried under the next twelve months of operational activity.

The first is whether the business generates enough cash from existing operations to fund the growth planned for FY27, or whether that growth requires external capital each time it happens. There is no wrong answer, but there is a material difference in the funding strategy each requires.

The second is whether current banking facilities, in their structure, size, and pricing, still fit the business being run. Facilities drawn up two or three years ago often reflect the business at that earlier point. Revenue has grown, the working capital cycle has changed, and the covenant positions may be tighter or looser than they should be. An independent

>Banking and Facility Review gives a clear read on whether the current structure is working for the business or simply the arrangement that has not yet been revisited.

The third is whether equity is the right instrument for where the business is headed. Debt has a carrying cost and a serviceability test; it fits businesses with predictable cash flows and manageable capital requirements. When growth is outpacing what cash flow can comfortably support, or when the business needs the kind of capital that brings strategic partners alongside it, the instrument question matters more than the amount.

Why Facility Readiness Starts Now

Banks and non-bank lenders both look at completed financial accounts when assessing new or increased facilities. For most SMEs, those accounts are the twelve months just finished, and the strongest application window opens immediately after EOFY when the data is fresh and the forward plan for the new year is clear.

If your business needs a facility in place by September or October, when seasonal cash needs or growth expenditure typically land, the structuring and application work needs to start now. Waiting until October to begin a facility conversation means waiting until late November or December for the outcome, which is rarely when you want it.

For businesses exploring capital beyond what their existing bank can offer, understanding what a capital raise preparation process involves before committing to one gives the business a clearer picture of the instruments available and what investors or lenders will actually require, before any process begins.

The end of June is genuinely busy. But the capital conversation had this week, before the new year's numbers start arriving, is one of the highest-return uses of a business owner's time at this point in the year. For business owners across Queensland and the East Coast who want to start FY27 with a clear picture of their financial position and what they need in place to fund what comes next, booking a discovery call this week puts that conversation in place before the new year begins.

faqs.q: How do my June 30 accounts affect my ability to borrow?
faqs.a: Lenders use your most recent completed annual accounts as the primary basis for assessing new or increased facilities. The closer you are to June 30, the more current your data is, which strengthens the application. Key figures lenders examine include your operating cash flow, net working capital position, existing debt levels, and the ratio of earnings to interest and principal commitments. Strong completed accounts, presented clearly with a forward plan, give you the best negotiating position for the year ahead.

faqs.q: What does a banking facility review examine in my business?
faqs.a: A Banking and Facility Review looks at whether your existing facilities, in their structure, pricing, and size, still suit the business you are running. It examines your current limits against actual peak usage, the covenants you are operating under, the interest rate and fee structure against current market, and whether the facility types you hold match the cash flow patterns of the business. Many businesses find they are either paying for more than they use or constrained by limits set for an earlier version of the business.

faqs.q: When should a growing Australian SME start a capital raise process?
faqs.a: The most common mistake is starting too late, after the cash need becomes urgent. By the time a raise becomes urgent, the business is negotiating from a weaker position and the timeline is compressed. Understanding what capital raise preparation involves well before a raise is needed allows the business to choose the right instrument, prepare the right materials, and approach the right funders from a position of choice rather than necessity. Six to twelve months of lead time is the standard for a well-run process.

faqs.q: How do I know if my current bank facilities are still structured correctly?
faqs.a: The clearest signal is a mismatch between your facility limits and your actual peak usage across the year. If you regularly approach your limit during busy periods or find you are paying line fees on capacity you never draw, the structure has drifted out of alignment with the business. Other signals include covenants that are consistently tight, a facility type that does not match how you use the money, or interest rates that have not been reviewed since the facility was first established.

faqs.q: What is the difference between debt and equity funding for Australian SMEs?
faqs.a: Debt requires regular repayment and carries an interest cost; it suits businesses with predictable cash flows that can service the commitment without compressing the working capital position. Equity does not require repayment but dilutes ownership and typically involves an investor who expects a return through growth or eventual sale. The right choice depends on the growth profile of the business, the predictability of its cash flows, and whether the owner wants a financial partner involved in decisions. Most SMEs benefit from a clear view of both options before committing to either.

faqs.q: How does working capital change affect borrowing capacity for Queensland businesses?
faqs.a: Lenders assess your ability to service debt based on your operating cash flow after accounting for working capital requirements. If your working capital is growing faster than your earnings, it reduces the free cash flow available to support debt commitments, which in turn limits what a lender is comfortable extending. For businesses with growing revenue but tightening cash, a cash flow review often reveals whether the pressure is structural or a timing issue that can be resolved before a facility application is made.

faqs.q: How can a fractional CFO support capital planning for a Brisbane or Queensland business?
faqs.a: A fractional CFO brings the capital markets perspective that most owner-led SMEs do not have in-house: knowing what lenders and investors actually look for, preparing the business and its financial story accordingly, and managing the facility or raise process alongside the day-to-day demands of the business. For businesses approaching a significant capital decision, whether a facility review, a growth raise, or a refinance, having that experience at the table before the first conversation with a bank or funder materially changes the outcome.
